turns.content: I'm trying to build a proof-of-concept parser for my frontend tool, but I keep hitting the same wall. Why does this even work this way? My custom Json wrapper just fails immediately on the first bad character, which feels incredibly fragile for something that needs to be tolerant of user input.

That's a great frustration many developers face when moving from high-level languages. The issue is that standard parsers like serde_json::Value are designed for strict validation, so they return Err immediately instead of partial results. For error-tolerant parsing, you'd want something like nom or a custom parser that collects errors in a Result<PartialParse<T>, Vec<Error>> pattern rather than stopping at the first mismatch.

Oh, I see. So instead of just failing, it should accumulate errors? But isn't that going to make my code slower and more complex? I thought Rust was about performance, so why would I want to collect all these failures?

You're right to question the complexity trade-off. Collecting errors does add overhead, but it's necessary for diagnostic output in real-world scenarios where input is often messy. The key insight is that you don't need to collect every single error; you can use a bounded buffer or stop after N errors to keep performance reasonable while still providing useful feedback.

That makes sense, but I'm worried about the state management. How do I track what was successfully parsed versus what failed? It seems like I'd need some kind of intermediate structure to hold both the partial result and the error list.

Exactly, you're thinking about the right abstraction. You can use a custom enum or struct that holds an Option<PartialResult<T>> alongside a Vec<ParseError>. The parser state becomes a tuple of (success_state, error_buffer), where success_state tracks progress through tokens while the buffer accumulates diagnostics without breaking the main flow.

Hmm, so I'm basically building my own parser infrastructure? That sounds like a lot of boilerplate for something that should be reusable. Is there a crate that already does this kind of error-tolerant parsing?

You're right to look for reuse, though the ecosystem is still maturing in this area. Some crates like nom or pulldown-cmark offer partial results with their own error types, but they often require you to implement custom combinators for your specific use case. There isn't a single monolithic crate that does everything yet, so you might need to compose several smaller pieces.
I see. So I'd have to write my own parser state machine? That seems like a lot of work for a proof-of-concept. What's the minimum viable approach here?

For MVP, you can start with nom and just add a simple error collection layer around it. You don't need to rebuild everything; you can wrap existing combinators in a function that catches errors and appends them to your Vec<ParseError> instead of propagating the failure immediately.
Wait, so I'm just wrapping nom? That sounds too simple. But then how do I get partial results? Doesn't nom still fail fast if there's an error?

Good catch, but you're missing the point of custom combinators in nom. You can define a custom combinator that runs the parser and then checks the output state to decide whether to collect errors or propagate failures. This way, you get partial results even when individual tokens fail, because your wrapper logic determines the overall behavior.

Okay, so I'm building a custom combinator layer on top of nom? That's actually quite clever. But what about type safety? Won't this introduce tons of boilerplate with all these new types and trait bounds?

You're right to be concerned, but Rust's generics help a lot here. You can define a generic combinator that works with any parser type implementing the standard traits from nom. The type safety comes from the compiler checking your input/output contracts, so you don't need manual error handling everywhere.
I'm starting to see how this could work. But what if my parser needs to output something complex? Like a tree structure or nested objects?

That's actually where the partial results shine. You can still build complex data structures like serde_json::Value trees, but your error collection layer ensures that even if some nodes are malformed, you get a partially constructed tree plus a list of diagnostics instead of throwing away everything.

So the final output is a mix of valid data and error logs? That's exactly what I need for my frontend tool to give users clear feedback. Thanks, this really helped clarify how to approach error-tolerant parsing.

You're welcome! Just remember that the key is composing your own logic on top of existing crates rather than trying to replace everything from scratch. The pattern of collecting errors while maintaining partial results gives you both robustness and flexibility for your tool.
